I don't just analyse data.
I find the story the numbers
are trying to tell.

Most analysts produce charts. I produce arguments — backed by data, structured as research, and written to reach the people who need to act on them.

My two independent research projects — covering $4.17B in Nigerian startup funding and 10,030 rental listings across six cities — were built entirely on my own initiative, using self-collected data, rigorous cross-validation, and published findings that challenged dominant market narratives.

I bring the discipline of a researcher, the communication skills of a writer, and the technical toolkit of a practising data analyst. That combination is rare.

What sets me apart
01
I challenge the consensus
My startup research exposed that CleanTech and HealthTech achieve 90% and 81.5% survival rates on a fraction of Fintech's capital — directly contradicting the dominant market narrative. I don't confirm what people already think. I find what the data actually says.
02
I own the full workflow
From web scraping raw listings in Python, through cleaning and modelling in SQL and Excel, to building interactive Power BI dashboards and publishing written analysis — I don't hand off to other people. I execute the complete data analyst workflow end to end.
03
I translate data into decisions
My findings aren't buried in appendices — they're written to reach founders, investors, and policy makers. I turn statistical findings into clear business arguments. That's a skill most technical analysts don't have, and most writers can't replicate.
Published Research
Study 01 · 2025 · Published on LinkedIn & Tableau Public
$4.17B in Nigerian startup funding tracked
Power BI Tableau Python SQL Excel
Project 01 / Nigerian Startup Ecosystem

The business model beats the sector — every time.

"CleanTech and HealthTech delivered 90% and 81.5% survival rates on a fraction of Fintech's capital — directly challenging the dominant market narrative."
"Across 8 years, 8 sectors, and 72 subsectors one pattern holds consistently — business model beats sector every time. The missing ingredient is not funding. It is discipline in how that funding is deployed."

Cross-validated $4.17B in funding across 293 companies, 528 deals, 8 sectors and 72 subsectors — resolving naming inconsistencies, currency discrepancies and unverified claims from TechCabal, Techpoint Africa, Crunchbase and LinkedIn.

  • Identified the central finding that sector label predicts almost nothing — business model predicts survival
  • Built 3 interactive Tableau dashboards covering funding trends, sector survival, subsector breakdowns, and investor behaviour
  • Automated repetitive validation workflows in Python, significantly reducing manual processing time
  • Published findings reached investment professionals, founders and startup intelligence organisations across Africa and Europe
Dashboard & Full Report ⬇ Download PDF
View on Tableau Public →
Study 02 · 2026 · Published on LinkedIn
97.6% of Lagos/Abuja market inaccessible to bottom 80%
Power BI Python/BeautifulSoup Excel NBS NLSS Data
Project 02 / Nigeria Housing Affordability

The crisis is real — but it's not where people think it is.

"Remove Lagos and Abuja and Nigeria's housing market looks fundamentally different. The crisis is manufactured by where the country concentrates its economic power."

Scraped 10,030 live rental listings from two independent platforms across six cities, applied the 30% housing cost standard against NBS NLSS income quintile data, and built a city-tier framework that reframes the national narrative.

  • Developed a four-tier city classification: Crisis (Lagos/Abuja) → Stressed → Functional → Accessible (Benin City)
  • Used dual-source scraping (NPC + Jiji.ng) for cross-validation — both platforms returned the same pattern
  • Built interactive Power BI dashboard integrating listing data, income quintiles, neighbourhood coordinates and affordability gap analysis
  • Findings carry direct policy implications: national housing interventions designed around Lagos will fail every other city
Power BI Dashboard & Full Report ⬇ Download PDF
View full analysis →
Project 03 · 2026 · Live Web App
10,030 real listings powering every recommendation
Bolt.new JavaScript Scraped Dataset Python
Project 03 / Nigeria Rent Affordability Advisor

From research to a tool anyone can actually use.

"Most analysts stop at the insight. This project went one step further — turning the housing research into a live product that puts the data directly in the hands of renters."

Built on the same 10,030-listing dataset from the housing affordability study, this interactive web app takes a user's income and city, and recommends specific neighbourhoods where rent is realistically affordable — applying the same 30% housing cost standard used in the research.

  • Converts a static research finding into a practical decision-making tool for real renters across six Nigerian cities
  • Powered entirely by self-scraped, real-world listing data — not synthetic or assumed pricing
  • Demonstrates the ability to go beyond dashboards and ship a working product from original research
  • Built and deployed using Bolt.new — from concept to live URL without a traditional dev pipeline
Try the live app →
How I work

I treat every dataset as a primary source — not a starting assumption.

My background is deliberately cross-disciplinary: an MSc in Logistics & Supply Chain Management from the University of Salford, years of analytical writing and market research, and formal training in Power BI, Tableau, SQL, Python and Excel.

That combination means I approach data problems differently. I'm trained to think about systems, flows and structures — which is why I look for the patterns beneath the numbers, not just the numbers themselves.

I use AI-assisted workflows daily (Claude, ChatGPT) to accelerate research structuring, documentation and cross-referencing — which means I produce analyst-grade output faster than typical workflows allow.

And because I can write — clearly, for non-technical audiences — my analysis doesn't stop at the dashboard. It becomes a brief, a report, a published argument that stakeholders can actually use.
